description: The GPIO peripheral clock is disabled after reset and must be enabled through the device Power and Sleep Controller (PSC) before any GPIO register accesses have effect. The GPIO input clock derives from the CPU clock (CPU/6 on this device), so PSC enable brings the internal logic to a known and timed state. Software shall execute the PSC transition sequence and verify completion prior to configuring direction or data. Attempting GPIO access while clock‑gated yields nonfunctional external behavior and misleading diagnostics. This enable step should be performed early in platform initialization and validated with a read of a known register (e.g., PID).
dependencies: PSC domain active; GPIO LPSC transitioned to ENABLE
Remarks: Gate all GPIO operations behind a verified PSC enable and log failures with actionable error codes.

feature_name: Pin multiplexing selection for GPIO pins
description: C6452 package pins are multiplexed; software must select the GPIO function for each pin intended for GPIO operation. Incorrect or omitted pinmux leaves pins connected to alternate peripherals, risking contention or no effect from GPIO writes. Configure only the required pins and confirm the mapping against the board schematic. Apply pinmux prior to any direction or output programming to avoid unintended transients on external nets. Read back or otherwise validate the final mux configuration before enabling interrupts or outputs.
evidence: SPRUF95 §2.3 (Pin Multiplexing); SPRS371 (Device Data Manual pin tables)
dependencies: Pinmux control registers programmed for selected pins; board constraints reviewed
Remarks: Centralize pinmux ownership to prevent conflicting configurations across subsystems.

description: Each GPIO bit is independently configured as input (DIRn=1) or output (DIRn=0) using DIR01. For outputs, set a benign level before enabling external interaction to minimize glitches at the pad. For inputs, sample via IN_DATA01 and apply system‑level debounce/qualification as needed. When toggling a pin between roles, sequence level changes to a safe state before switching DIR to avoid transients. Maintain a software shadow of DIR01 to support atomic and audited updates across contexts.
dependencies: PSC enabled; target pins selected by pinmux
Remarks: Batch direction updates with masks and verify with a read‑back compare in debug builds.

description: Use SET_DATA01 and CLR_DATA01 to change output levels atomically without read‑modify‑write hazards. These registers update only the bits written as 1, preserving other outputs even under ISR preemption. Prefer them over OUT_DATA01 when adjusting subsets of outputs to improve determinism. After updates, a non‑destructive read (e.g., CLR_DATA01 readback semantics) can confirm driven state. Maintain precomputed masks for common output groups to streamline critical sections.
dependencies: DIR01 configured for outputs; GPIO clock enabled
Remarks: Adopt atomic set/clear APIs and restrict OUT_DATA01 full‑port writes to single‑owner contexts.

description: OUT_DATA01 determines the level driven on pins configured as outputs (DIRn=0). Reading OUT_DATA01 returns the register content, which may differ from the actual pin level under certain board conditions; use IN_DATA01 if true pin level is needed. Writes to OUT_DATA01 have no effect on bits configured as inputs. Initialize outputs to a known state via OUT_DATA01 or, preferably, atomic SET/CLR writes. Ensure that pinmux and PSC are valid before performing OUT_DATA01 writes to avoid undefined pad behavior.
dependencies: PSC enabled; DIR01 outputs selected
Remarks: Use IN_DATA01 to verify external level when signal integrity or pull configurations can affect pad state.

description: IN_DATA01 provides the instantaneous logic level observed at each GPIO pin. Software should mask reads to the subset of interest and handle any necessary debounce or qualification in software or hardware. Because sampling is synchronous to the peripheral clock, a small latency relative to external transitions is expected. Validate input polarity against board wiring prior to deployment. For time‑critical capture, consider interrupt or EDMA‑assisted schemes instead of tight polling.
dependencies: PSC enabled; pins configured as inputs via DIR01
Remarks: Document expected active level and qualification method per signal to avoid misinterpretation.

description: Per‑pin rising‑edge detection is enabled by writing 1s to SET_RIS_TRIG01 and disabled via CLR_RIS_TRIG01. Configure edge detection only after clearing any stale interrupt status to prevent spurious first interrupts. Route the resulting interrupt to the CPU as required and enable the corresponding mask bits. Validate behavior with known transitions and ensure ISR service can keep pace with expected rates. Keep enables tightly limited to active signals to minimize IRQ load.
dependencies: INTSTAT01 cleared; interrupt routing prepared; BINTEN/CPU enables configured
Remarks: Always clear latched status before enabling and confirm ISR clears both module and CPU flags.

description: Per‑pin falling‑edge detection is enabled with SET_FAL_TRIG01 and disabled with CLR_FAL_TRIG01. Align the selected edge with external circuitry polarity to capture intended transitions. As with rising edges, clear preexisting status before activation to avoid false triggers. Ensure interrupt routing and CPU enables are in place before unmasking. Validate across operating conditions to confirm robustness.
dependencies: INTSTAT01 cleared; interrupt routing prepared; BINTEN/CPU enables configured
Remarks: Match edge polarity to board signalling and record rationale for maintainability.

description: Interrupt status for GPIO is latched per bit in INTSTAT01 and is cleared by writing a 1 to the serviced bit. Software should read INTSTAT01 to identify asserting sources and then clear all serviced bits before ISR exit. Failing to clear the latch (and any CPU‑level IFR bits when applicable) can cause re‑entry or masked future events. Establish an ordered read/clear discipline to avoid race conditions under bursty activity. Instrument servicing to detect overruns early in bring‑up.
dependencies: Interrupt path enabled; ISR registered; CPU IFR handling in place (for direct routes)
Remarks: Use strict masks for W1C writes and validate end‑to‑end in unit tests.

description: Bank interrupts are enabled by setting bits in BINTEN to allow GPIO banks to request service. Combine this with per‑pin edge enables to control which signals can generate interrupts. Ensure CPU‑side enables and vector routing are configured to accept the bank request. When multiple pins assert within a bank, software must iterate through all set bits in INTSTAT01. Keep bank enables aligned with active configuration to prevent stray IRQs.
dependencies: Edge enables configured; CPU vector enables set
Remarks: Audit BINTEN and edge masks together as part of runtime reconfiguration.

feature_name: Banked interrupt aggregation and source resolution
description: GPIO pins are grouped into banks that aggregate to a bank interrupt line. On interrupt entry, software reads INTSTAT01 to determine which pin(s) within the bank asserted. Multiple bits may be set simultaneously and must be serviced without loss. Clear all handled bits before returning to avoid missing coalesced events. Choose vector priorities based on expected event rates and latency constraints.
dependencies: BINTEN enabled; edge triggers configured; CPU vector mapped
Remarks: Design ISRs to drain all active sources per entry and budget worst‑case service time.

feature_name: Direct GPIO interrupt lines to CPU (single‑signal)
description: Some GPIO signals can be routed directly to distinct CPU interrupt lines, reducing identification overhead and latency. Availability and exact mapping are device‑specific and referenced in the device data manual. When used, service requires clearing both GPIO INTSTAT01 and the CPU IFR for the selected vector. This path is recommended for latency‑critical signals where vectors are available. Validate mapping at boot and guard enablement behind device checks.
dependencies: Interrupt line mapped; CPU IER/IFR configured; INTSTAT01 handling in ISR
Remarks: Confirm per‑pin direct route support on the target and document vector usage to avoid conflicts.

feature_name: EDMA event generation from GPIO edges
description: GPIO edges can be used to generate synchronization events for the EDMA controller. This enables low‑latency or high‑rate signaling paths without tight CPU polling, e.g., timestamp capture or buffered sampling. Correctly configure EDMA channels and event mapping per device documentation. Coordinate GPIO status clearing with EDMA completion to avoid missed or repeated triggers. Validate throughput and error handling under sustained loads.
evidence: SPRUF95 §2.11 (EDMA Event Support); SPRS371 (EDMA event mapping)
dependencies: EDMA present and configured; event crossbar/mapping programmed
Remarks: Size buffers for burst worst case and implement completion/error callbacks.

description: A robust initialization order is: enable PSC for GPIO, configure pin multiplexing, program DIR01 for roles, set initial outputs atomically, clear INTSTAT01, configure edge triggers, enable BINTEN and CPU vectors. This sequence minimizes spurious events and output glitches. Make the routine idempotent so it can safely re‑run after subsystem resets. Read back key registers after each phase to verify state. Log failures with enough context to aid bring‑up and field diagnostics.
dependencies: PSC enabled; pinmux set; DIR programmed; INTSTAT01 cleared
Remarks: Encapsulate the sequence in a single API with clear pre/post conditions and asserts in debug builds.

description: GPIO functional behavior is independent of device endianness. Software should still honor device endianness when constructing multi‑byte values in shared structures, but GPIO bit operations and register protocols are unaffected. This simplifies portability of GPIO drivers across endianness configurations. Validate only that MMIO accessors match system endianness expectations. No special GPIO‑specific byte swapping is required.
dependencies: System endianness configured; MMIO accessors correct
Remarks: Keep GPIO accessors simple bitwise operations; avoid unnecessary byte‑swap logic.

description: During emulation halts or breakpoints, asynchronous GPIO events may occur while the CPU is stopped. Upon resume, software should check and clear INTSTAT01 to reconcile any pending events. Take care when single‑stepping around W1C sequences to avoid unintended clears. Instrument debug builds to trace ISR entries relative to halt/resume cycles. Coordinate with emulator settings to minimize perturbation of interrupt timing.
dependencies: Debug session active; ISR and status handling aware of halts
Remarks: Add post‑resume hygiene to clear stale status and resynchronize software state.

description: The GPIO module does not provide built‑in debounce or input qualification. Mechanical switches or noisy signals must be debounced in software or via external hardware. Sampling cadence and filter constants should reflect the worst‑case bounce profile of the source. Excessively tight polling can alias glitches; prefer interrupts or hardware filtering for clean detection. Document the chosen strategy per input to ensure consistent behavior.
evidence: SPRUF95 functional description (no debounce feature described)
dependencies: External filtering or software debounce in place
Remarks: Implement a reusable debounce utility and validate against representative stimuli.

description: When the GPIO module is placed into reduced‑power states via PSC, clocked operations such as edge detection and interrupt generation stop. Upon wake, software may need to re‑apply configuration or at least verify that previous settings persist. Sequence re‑enable, status clear, and edge re‑arming to avoid spurious interrupts. If low‑power transitions are frequent, measure the re‑init latency budget. Ensure outputs return to safe states immediately after power restoration.
dependencies: PSC transitions orchestrated; re‑initialization path implemented
Remarks: Treat low‑power exit as a mini‑init and audit for spurious edges.

description: The PID register provides a read‑only peripheral identification and revision field. Reading PID early verifies clocking and address mapping to the GPIO block. Use PID to gate subsequent initialization if an unexpected value is observed. This aids in diagnosing misrouted pinmux or clock configuration issues. Log the PID value during bring‑up for traceability.
dependencies: PSC enabled; base address correct
Remarks: Include PID in startup diagnostics to confirm peripheral presence and revision.

feature_name: Base address and offsets for MMIO access
description: GPIO registers are memory‑mapped at a device‑specific base address with defined offsets for each register. Correct base computation and volatile MMIO access are prerequisites for reliable operation. Use documented offsets (e.g., DIR01 at 0x10, BINTEN at 0x08) from the user’s guide. Validate the base address from the device data manual before any access. Misaddressing can lead to silent failures or unintended peripheral modification.
dependencies: SoC memory map known; MMIO infrastructure available
Remarks: Abstract base/offsets behind typed accessors to avoid hard‑coded literals.

description: The GPIO documentation does not describe module‑local security or privilege enforcement such as access permissions or lock bits. Consequently, access control is a system‑level responsibility (e.g., MPU/MMU regions or secure boot policy). Restrict GPIO register programming to trusted code paths and validate configuration at runtime. Avoid exposing raw MMIO to unprivileged contexts. Document any platform‑specific protections applied around GPIO usage.
evidence: SPRUF95 (no security features stated); SPRS371 (no GPIO‑specific security noted)
dependencies: System privilege model (MPU/MMU/secure world) enforced
Remarks: Apply least‑privilege to MMIO and audit GPIO writes in critical modules.

description: The GPIO input path includes synchronization to the peripheral clock domain to stabilize asynchronous external signals. This introduces a bounded latency between an external transition and observation via IN_DATA01. During tight timing analysis or emulation, account for this latency to avoid misattribution of delays. Do not assume sub‑cycle timing visibility at the pin from software. Validate timing with external measurement when needed.
evidence: SPRUF95 Figure 1 (GPIO Peripheral Block Diagram); functional description
dependencies: PSC clock stable; external signal integrity ensured
Remarks: Include synchronizer latency in timing budgets and verify empirically where critical.

description: The GPIO module’s input clock is derived as CPU/6 on this device, setting the timing granularity for internal sampling and edge detection. Software that depends on precise timing (e.g., polling cadence) should align with this clock relationship. This ratio also informs worst‑case ISR arrival skew relative to the CPU clock. Validate that system clock changes propagate as expected to the GPIO domain. Avoid tight loops that assume pin change visibility faster than the peripheral clock permits.
dependencies: Device CPU clock configured and stable
Remarks: Base polling/timeout parameters on the documented peripheral clock ratio.
